disease.name: Pulmonary Embolism
disease.note: PE is a life-threatening cardiovascular emergency caused by thrombotic occlusion of pulmonary arteries. Clinical probability assessment (Wells score/PERC rule) guides D-dimer testing. CTPA is the gold standard diagnostic. Anticoagulation is mainstay; thrombolysis or embolectomy for massive PE.

immediateDangerSigns.description: McConnell sign, D-shaped LV, RV/ LV ratio >1, elevated PAP

additionalCriteria.description: Simplified PESI: age >80, cancer, chronic cardiopulmonary disease, HR ≥110, SBP <100, SpO2 <90%
Saddle embolus or multiple lobar emboli

qualitativeModifiers.description: Central chest pain may indicate RV ischaemia

historyFeatures.note: Blood-streaked sputum due to pulmonary infarction
Suggests massive PE with haemodynamic compromise
DVT symptoms; present in ~50% of PE patients

examFeatures.note: Unilateral leg swelling, erythema, Homan's sign

riskFactors.note: Highest risk: hip/knee arthroplasty, pelvic surgery, neurosurgery
Prolonged bed rest, long-haul air travel >6 hours
Especially adenocarcinoma, pancreatic, lung, brain, haematological
Factor V Leiden, prothrombin mutation, antiphospholipid, protein C/S/AT deficiency

subtypes.name: Massive (High-Risk) PE
subtypes.management: Immediate reperfusion: systemic thrombolysis (alteplase 100 mg over 2 h) unless contraindicated; catheter-directed thrombolysis or surgical embolectomy if contraindicated; IV unfractionated heparin

subtypes.name: Submassive (Intermediate-Risk) PE
subtypes.management: Anticoagulation with LMWH or DOAC; monitor for haemodynamic decompensation; consider rescue thrombolysis if deterioration

subtypes.name: Low-Risk PE
subtypes.management: DOAC (apixaban, rivaroxaban, edoxaban, dabigatran); early discharge possible if stable

bedside.name: ECG
bedside.note: Sinustachycardia most common; S1Q3T3 (classic but insensitive); RV strain (RBBB, TWI V1-V4)

bedside.name: PERC rule
bedside.indication: PERC-negative patients need no further testing (age >50, HR >100, SpO2 <95%, prior DVT/PE, surgery, hemoptysis, oestrogen, unilateral leg swelling)

laboratory.name: D-dimer (high-sensitivity ELISA)
laboratory.indication: Negative D-dimer (age-adjusted: age × 0.1 mg/L if >50) rules out PE in low/moderate probability
laboratory.note: Specificity decreases with age, malignancy, pregnancy, hospitalisation

laboratory.name: Troponin I or T
laboratory.indication: elevated in RV injury; prognostic marker for intermediate-risk PE

laboratory.name: NT-proBNP or BNP
laboratory.indication: elevated in RV strain; prognostic marker

imaging.name: CT pulmonary angiogram (CTPA)
imaging.indication: moderate/high Wells or positive D-dimer; contraindications include severe contrast allergy, renal impairment
imaging.note: Bilateral pulmonary artery filling defects confirm PE; also assess RV/LV ratio

imaging.name: V/Q scan
imaging.indication: good lung function, normal CXR; report as low/intermediate/high probability
imaging.note: Pregnancy: consider V/Q or low-dose CTPA

imaging.name: Chest X-ray
imaging.indication: rule out pneumonia, pneumothorax; normal CXR with hypoxia raises PE suspicion

advanced.name: Echocardiogram (transthoracic)
advanced.indication: assess for RV strain (McConnell sign, D-shaped LV, RV/LV ratio, PAP); rule out other causes of shock
advanced.note: Normal echo does NOT rule out PE

advanced.name: Lower limb compression ultrasound (CUS)
advanced.indication: if equivocal CT but high suspicion
advanced.note: Proximal DVT on CUS confirms VTE and justifies anticoagulation

management.protocolReference: ESC Guidelines on Acute Pulmonary Embolism (2019/2024 update); BTS Guidelines; ATS/ERS Consensus

pathways.criteria: normotensive, sPESI 0, no RV dysfunction, troponin negative
treatment: DOAC first-line: apixaban 10 mg BID × 7 days then 5 mg BID; OR rivaroxaban 15 mg BID × 21 days then 20 mg daily
Alternative: LMWH bridge to warfarin (INR 2–3) or dabigatran/edoxaban after 5 days of parenteral therapy
Early discharge possible if home circumstances adequate
Risk factor modification: assess for reversible triggers, smoking cessation, weight loss
pathways.followUp: Review at 2 weeks; anticoagulation minimum 3 months; consider extended if unprovoked

pathways.criteria: normotensive BUT RV dysfunction (echo or CT) AND/OR elevated troponin/BNP
treatment: DOAC (apixaban or rivaroxaban) OR LMWH (enoxaparin 1.5 mg/kg SC daily or 1 mg/kg BID) bridging to warfarin if preferred
Monitor for haemodynamic deterioration: BP, HR, SpO2, urine output q4h
Echocardiogram within 24–48 h to reassess RV function
If deterioration (hypotension, shock, worsening RV): consider rescue thrombolysis (alteplase 50 mg IV bolus or 100 mg over 2 h)
ICU/HDU admission if significant RV dysfunction
pathways.escalation: Rescue thrombolysis for haemodynamic decompensation. Surgical or catheter embolectomy if thrombolysis contraindicated or fails.

pathways.criteria: obstructive shock, persistent hypotension, or cardiac arrest due to PE
treatment: Immediate multidisciplinary team activation (respiratory, cardiology, ICU, radiology)
Systemic thrombolysis: alteplase 100 mg IV over 2 h (or 50 mg bolus if cardiac arrest)
If thrombolysis contraindicated: catheter-directed thrombolysis (ultrasound-assisted CDT) or surgical embolectomy (cardiopulmonary bypass)
Unfractionated heparin IV bolus (80 U/kg) then infusion (18 U/kg/h, target APTT 1.5–2.5x normal)
ICU management: invasive BP monitoring, inotropes if needed (noradrenaline), fluid cautious (RV preload dependent)
Mechanical ventilation if respiratory failure: low tidal volumes, avoid high PEEP
pathways.escalation: Thrombolysis within 2 hours of diagnosis if high-risk. Embolectomy if thrombolysis contraindicated or fails.

complications.management: Reversal agents (andexanet for DOAC; PCC for warfarin); assess risk-benefit of resuming

disease.emergencyPriority: HIGH - massive PE with shock requires immediate thrombolysis; intermediate-risk with RV strain requires close monitoring
diagnosticClues: acute dyspnoea + pleuritic chest pain + haemoptysis
McConnell sign on echo (RV free wall hypokinesis with apical sparing)
exclusionClues: normal CTPA or low-probability V/Q scan
alternative diagnosis confirmed (e.g., pneumonia, pneumothorax on CXR)
